topic: A person learns their college is closing down
raw_output:
[story 1]

The official notice, stark white against the beige wall of the student union, felt like a physical blow. Elias traced the embossed lettering with a fingertip, the paper cool and unforgiving. He leaned back in the worn armchair, the springs protesting beneath his weight with a low, mournful creak. Outside, the autumn rain hammered against the windowpane, mirroring the relentless drumming in his chest. He didn't move to look out; the world outside seemed muted, a watercolor washed out by pervasive apathy. Every familiar corner of the campus—the quad where he used to sketch, the library where he’d wrestled with philosophy—suddenly felt hollow, a stage set being dismantled. He just sat, staring at the dust motes dancing in the weak afternoon light, wishing he could somehow dissolve into the silence.

[story 2]

"So, that's it," Sarah mumbled, her voice barely audible over the sound of the campus maintenance crew efficiently packing away furniture. She kicked at a loose pebble on the pavement, the sound sharp and pointless. She kept running a hand through her hair, the strands feeling heavy and damp against her temples. The vibrant energy that had fueled her last few semesters had evaporated, leaving behind a strange, heavy lethargy. She stopped by the old oak tree near the entrance, sinking onto the rough bark. She didn't cry; the tears felt too large, too dramatic for the sheer, dull weight settling over her shoulders. She just let her gaze drift toward the ground, watching the fallen leaves decompose, feeling utterly disconnected from the impending void.

[story 3]

I kept re-reading the departmental memos, the words blurring into meaningless shapes. My hands felt perpetually cold, even under my thick sweater. Each page turned was an act of deliberate resistance, a futile attempt to hold onto something tangible when everything around me was dissolving into bureaucratic jargon. I paced my small dorm room, the rhythm of my footsteps echoing too loudly in the emptiness. I stopped by the window, pulling the curtain aside just enough to see the sprawling, familiar campus grounds, now looking strangely vacant, like a photograph left out in the sun. A deep sigh escaped me, a sound that seemed to carry the exhaustion of a thousand unsaid goodbyes. There was nothing left to fight for, only the slow, creeping realization of absence.
